decision_context.trigger: Pedestrian detected in vehicle path by LiDAR at approximately 5.6 seconds before impact
available_inputs: HD map with lane geometry and road boundaries
Vehicle speed and trajectory data (38 mph in 35 mph zone)
alternatives_considered: Continue current trajectory (selected by motion planner)
Initiate emergency braking (disabled by design for Volvo XC90 platform)
Execute evasive steering maneuver (not implemented in motion planner)
constraints_operative: Volvo factory emergency braking system disabled to avoid conflicts with Uber ATG autonomous driving system
System not designed to alert safety driver when intervention is needed
Operational design domain: urban arterial road, nighttime, 35 mph speed limit
decision_context._recoverability_note: Recoverable: operational design context was well-documented in Uber ATG engineering records and NTSB investigation. The system's design constraints (disabled emergency braking, no operator alert) were architectural choices recorded in system documentation.

decision_logic.output: Continue current trajectory at 38 mph; no emergency action initiated

model_inference.model_id: Uber ATG perception and classification stack
model_inference.model_version: Version at time of March 2018 incident

decision_logic.rule_version: Uber ATG self-driving system perception and motion planning stack (version at time of incident)

parameters.perception_model: Deep learning object detection and classification
parameters.classification_behavior: Object reclassified 19 times in 5.6 seconds (vehicle, other, bicycle, other, bicycle...)
parameters.path_prediction: Each reclassification reset path prediction history; system could not maintain stable trajectory forecast
parameters.action_suppression: System suppressed emergency actions for approximately 1 second after each reclassification

thresholds.emergency_braking_trigger: disabled (by design on Volvo XC90 platform)

decision_logic._recoverability_note: Opaque: inference logs were formally complete -- every classification output was recorded -- but the reasoning behind each reclassification transition was epistemically irreducible. NTSB documented that perception outputs were logged without the internal model state that would explain why the system oscillated between classifications. This is the paradigmatic case of opacity-driven schema drift: the log schema captured outputs but not the operative decision logic.

external_dependencies: Volvo XC90 vehicle platform (factory safety systems disabled)
decision_boundary._recoverability_note: Partially recoverable: individual subsystems were documented, but the perception-to-planning handoff -- the critical boundary where classification instability translated into action suppression -- was not monitored as a governance boundary. NTSB found that no mechanism existed to assess whether the cross-subsystem interaction was adequate to the safety risks.

decision_quality_indicators._recoverability_note: Structurally unfillable: no confidence threshold existed for triggering protective action. The system produced classification outputs without uncertainty quantification that would have flagged 19 reclassifications in 5.6 seconds as anomalous. Ground truth (pedestrian presence on road) was available in sensor data but not surfaced as a quality indicator to the decision system.

human_override_record.override_decision: No override executed; safety driver was not monitoring the road
human_override_record.independence_assessment: Safety driver operated under organizational expectations to monitor autonomous system performance; NTSB found the driver was visually distracted
human_override_record._recoverability_note: Captured but diagnostically complex: the override record shows the safety driver did not intervene, and NTSB documented the distraction. However, even an attentive driver faced an architectural constraint: the system provided no alert that intervention was needed, and the time between final classification and impact (1.3 seconds) was at the boundary of human reaction capability.

temporal_metadata._recoverability_note: Recoverable: NTSB-mandated event data recording captured sensor inputs, perception outputs, and vehicle state at high temporal resolution. System logs were available immediately after the incident. Formal ground truth (NTSB probable cause determination, HAR-19/03) was published November 19, 2019. hash_chain and sequence_number are post-hoc placeholders.
